key_insight: The paper turns alloy short-range-order design into a differentiable optimization problem, enabling direct gradient-based search from target SRO to target mechanical property.
engineering_relevance: This gives materials engineers a faster, scalable way to design high-entropy alloys with desired stiffness while preserving thermodynamic realism.
method: Treats atomic occupancies as continuous variables so gradient descent can replace random-swap reverse Monte Carlo.
Adds a physics-based correction term to keep generated structures realistic across multi-element alloys.
Uses a small neural network to map composition and SRO statistics to mechanical properties, closing the design loop.
evidence_signal: Across nine FCC and BCC alloys, the workflow captured SRO-driven stiffness shifts from -20% to +57%, scaled better than random-swap search, and matched three of four Co-Cr-Ni target stiffness values within 6%.
watch_item: The approach depends on sufficiently large supercells; the abstract warns that 108-atom cells can give misleading trends and suggests at least 864 atoms for reliable direction and magnitude.
paper_title: Differentiable inverse design of short-range order in high-entropy alloys: from target sro to target property
